text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, and the surgeon uses a medial approach to expose the duodenum. The posterior surface of the duodenum is fully visible.

Following cocharization, the surgeon identifies the groove of the superior mesenteric vein. The bowel distal to the ligament of Treitz is divided using a surgical stapler, and the bowel is repositioned into the right upper quadrant.

In reverse Trendelenburg position, the surgeon drops the hepatic flexure and opens the lesser sac, revealing the anterior surface of the pancreas. The superior mesenteric vein is identified and skeletonized, exposing it clearly from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the gastrocolic trunk, then divides it, separating it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon visualizes the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck, with the confluence of veins clearly identified. A Nathanson liver retractor is inserted to elevate the liver, facilitating dissection superior to the pancreas.

The surgeon divides the stomach just proximal to the pylorus, with the pancreas clearly visible in the surgical field. The hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery are skeletonized and identified. After confirming the anatomical structures, the gastroduodenal artery is tied off and divided.

The surgeon confirms the development of the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck using a back scratcher. The Thunder Beat device is then employed to divide the pancreatic neck, with the pancreatic duct being sharply cut before completing the transection.

The surgeon views the confluence of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein, then begins to identify and isolate the bile duct from a lateral perspective, with a lymph node being visibly dropped down.

The surgeon visualizes the right hepatic artery and applies a bulldog clamp to control the bile duct, preventing excessive bile spillage. The bile duct is then divided, with the right hepatic artery preserved throughout the procedure.

The surgeon divides and separates the uncinate process from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) using surgical staplers, leaving a small cuff of tissue intact near the uncinate process as the lesion is well away from the SMA.

The surgeon places the resected specimen into a specimen retrieval bag for extraction through a fan and steel incision, preparing it for frozen section analysis. Subsequently, a cholecystectomy is performed, and the gallbladder is placed into a separate specimen retrieval bag.

The surgeon observes the surgical field, which is clean and dry, after sharply dividing the pancreas at the level of the duct. The pancreatic duct is difficult to visualize in this case, and the focus shifts to the reconstruction phase.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas from the branches of the celiac artery and the splenic vein posteriorly. Stay sutures are placed along the marginal vessels on the inferior and superior edges of the pancreas. Due to the obscured view of the pancreatic duct, the surgeon plans a pancreatic gastrostomy to the posterior wall of the stomach.

The surgeon performs a posterior gastrotomy using the Thunderbeat device, creating an incision in the posterior wall of the stomach. A purse string suture is then placed with full-thickness bites around the gastrotomy site, ensuring there are no gaps.

The surgeon makes a small incision in the staple line and uses the Thunderbeat to create another gastrotomy. A Maryland dissector is introduced through this gastrotomy and then through the posterior gastrotomy to pull the stay sutures, facilitating the invagination of the pancreas remnant into the stomach for the pancreatic gastrostomy.
The surgeon ties down the purse string suture tightly around the pancreas, which has been pulled through the posterior gastrotomy. Subsequently, the anterior gastrotomy is closed.

The surgeon orients the bowel from a lateral view of the right flank for the hepatico-jejunostomy. A single layer hand-sewn technique is employed using a monofilament 5-0 suture, followed by a ductomucosa anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis, confirming it is sound and well-oriented. A clean white sponge is used to check for any biliary leak, with no evidence of leakage observed.

The surgeon sets up an anti-colic gastrojejunostomy, creating a common channel with a stapler. The gastroenterostomy is closed in two layers using running braided 3-0 sutures, completing the final stitch. The anastomosis appears well-formed.
This patient did well.
